reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: Gotham Academy: First Year #5 strikes the perfect balance of Gotham bizarre and teen melodrama by telling a melodramatic teen story in a way that only makes sense in Gotham. Olive takes center stage and confronts her greatest fears in quick, exhausting succession. Gorgeous surreal art tells the story effectively and poignantly.
pros: Really, really pretty art
Exhaustingly paced (in a good way)
Tells a melodramatic teen story that can only happen in Gotham
cons: The ending is a bit of a deus ex machina

reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: Justice League: Dream Girls – A DC Pride Event #4 is exactly the kind of satisfying conclusion I was hoping I’d get. After four weeks of emotional swings from the two leads, we get a great “coming together” moment that highlights what makes these characters so resilient and reliable. Couple that with a satisfying apology from the Justice League and an absolutely astonishing backup from Klaus Janso…
pros: Great classic superhero story about belief in yourself and your friends
Well written dialogue with great posing from the artists
Important greater DC status quo implications
A wonderful new format for celebration issues
That Klaus Janson backup

reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: Wonder Woman #34 sees what’s presumably the final battle of the Wonder War and it’s a doozy. It’s beautifully brought to life by two artists whose styles clash a little bit when put together but there isn’t a single page that isn’t fun to look at. Tom King poses interesting questions but ultimately sets up a confusing ending that intrigues me as much as it frustrates me.
pros: Incredible action between Wonder Woman and Matriarch
Matriarch is an absolutely brutal character
cons: The two artists are great, but their styles clash a little bit
The ending is more frustrating than satisfying

reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: Zatanna #3 is an action-packed issue that sees Zatanna asserting her authority as Prime Magus. Jamal Campbell takes us through the magic realms of two different cultures in a beautiful way that goes against what we see in most forms of media.
pros: The character designs for Guede Nibo, Persephone and Mnemosyne are beautiful, incredibly detailed and in tune with their respective cultures
Jamal Campbell portrays Voodoo in a respectful and positive light
Zatanna casts her spells in a creative way using music when being silenced
cons: The pacing feels a little off in this issue, it doesn't feel as if it takes place in the same night as issue #2

reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: Gorgeously illustrated by Dan Mora and Fico Ossio, Justice League Unlimited #20 is an entertaining clash of egos. Mark Waid’s characterization of all the main players is the true star of this show. Waid’s Batman and Luthor are outstanding. However, none of this would be as successful without Dan Mora and Fico Ossio’s artwork.
pros: Waid's characterizations of each major player is spot on.
Waid's use of characterization and conflict due to ego to drive the narrative forward.
The artwork tells the story without superfluous exposition.
Dan Mora and Fico Ossio's artwork is gorgeous.
Tamra Bonvillain’s colors do an excellent job maintaining a visual consistency between the two artists.
cons: Although the artwork is excellent there are points where you can tell the differences between the two artists.
I can't wait to see more from the b-plot involving Powers Planet.

reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: Feral #24 is another exciting installment in a series that has never once taken its foot off the gas in terms of thrills and chills.
pros: Feral continues to be a thrilling and chilling horror series that refuses to play it safe.
The art is the perfect blend of cute and gruesome.
Climactic cliffhanger ending that sets up the highly anticipated meeting between the indoor cats and the stray dogs.

reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: I didn’t think I would get as emotional or fired up reading an issue of Void Rivals that focused squarely on Skuxxoid, but here we are. Void Rivals #31 delivered a really serious story about revolution, freedom, authoritarianism, the problems with old leadership, the fire of young progressives, and it did all that while being an entertaining brawl between identical looking characters you could som…
pros: Energetic art that makes telling Skuxxoids apart surprisingly easy
Interesting meanings you can take away from the book
Nice that it didn't matter to the greater Energon Universe (y'know, yet)
cons: This was more interesting than the entire Quintesson War and it's only one issue

reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: Hellboy and the B.P.R.D.: The Monster of Nivola #1 is a gorgeous one-shot that feels extremely comfortable for a lapsed Hellboy reader like myself to jump back into after years away. With a tight script, stunning art, energetic letters, a cool setting, and a powerful message, this Hellboy oneshot is a home run in every meaningful metric a comic fan like me cares about.
pros: Genuinely fantastic art
Incredibly cool setting
Just a wonderful premise for a one shot

reason.label: AIPT review
reason.snippet: Venom #259 succeeds because it never loses sight of its characters while preparing for something much larger. Ewing gives Dylan Brock the spotlight and uses him to anchor an issue packed with plot progression, emotional beats, and looming threats. The ensemble cast continues to feel purposeful, while Gomez's energetic artwork gives every major moment weight and spectacle. By the final page, the st…
pros: Dylan Brock receives some of his strongest characterization yet.
Al Ewing continues to weave long-term plot threads together skillfully.
Carlos Gomez and Frank D'Armata deliver striking action and memorable splash pages.
cons: Readers focused solely on Mary Jane may want more page time for Venom.
